steps.explanation: Writing $3\theta = 2\theta+\theta$ lets us use the tangent addition formula. Setting $t=\tan\theta$ keeps the algebra compact.
Using $\tan(A+B) = \dfrac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A\tan B}$ with $A=2\theta$, $B=\theta$ turns the triple angle into an expression in $\tan 2\theta$ and $\tan\theta$.

steps.description: Write tan 2theta in terms of t
steps.explanation: The double-angle formula for tangent lets us express everything through the single variable $t = \tan\theta$.
The top of the fraction is the sum of $\tan 2\theta$ and $\tan\theta$; we combine these over a common denominator.
Placing $t$ over the common denominator $1-t^2$ and expanding $2t + t - t^3$ gives $3t - t^3$ on top.
The bottom of the fraction is $1$ minus the product $\tan 2\theta\tan\theta$; multiplying the two tangents gives $\dfrac{2t^2}{1-t^2}$.
Writing $1$ as $\dfrac{1-t^2}{1-t^2}$ and subtracting collects the denominator into a single fraction with top $1-3t^2$.
The tangent of the triple angle is the combined numerator divided by the combined denominator.
Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal; the common factor $1-t^2$ then cancels neatly, top and bottom.
Replacing $t$ by $\tan\theta$ returns the identity in its required form.
At $\theta = 45^\circ$ the formula gives $-1$, which equals $\tan 135^\circ$, confirming the result.
At $\theta = 30^\circ$ the denominator vanishes, matching the fact that $\tan 90^\circ$ is undefined; the formula behaves correctly at this singular value too.
Every manipulation was a valid equality wherever the tangents are defined, so the identity holds.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Expanding $\tan(2\theta+\theta)$ and substituting $\tan 2\theta = \dfrac{2\tan\theta}{1-\tan^2\theta}$ gives, after the factor $1-\tan^2\theta$ cancels, $\tan 3\theta \equiv \dfrac{3\tan\theta - \tan^3\theta}{1 - 3\tan^2\theta}$.
